title: Cyber Security (Compounding of Offences) Regulations 2024
auditNote: Enabling section corrected 2026-03-04: original file cited s55 (protection against suits) throughout; s54 is the compounding power. The compoundable offences schedule in com-r3 has been corrected: s27 → s21 (risk assessment), s28 → s23 (audit), s43 → s44 (obstruction), s36 → s22 (codes of practice). Items (e) and (f) in com-r3 referenced s29 and s30 (licensing provisions) — these have been corrected to reference the risk assessment/audit subsections of s21 and s23 respectively, but the precise sub-section numbering requires gazette verification. Annual report reference in com-r12 (s58) also requires gazette verification.
subject: Specifies compoundable offences under the Act, prescribes the methodology for calculating compound amounts, establishes application and payment procedures, and defines the legal effect of compounding

provisions.title: Citation and Commencement
provisions.requirement: These regulations may be cited as the Cyber Security (Compounding of Offences) Regulations 2024 and shall come into operation on 26 August 2024.
provisions.explanation: Establishes the formal name and commencement date of these regulations, aligning with the parent Act.

provisions.title: Interpretation
provisions.requirement: In these Regulations, unless the context otherwise requires: 'compoundable offence' means an offence specified in the Schedule to these Regulations; 'compound amount' means the amount of money determined under regulation 5 as the sum payable for the compounding of an offence; 'compounding officer' means the Chief Executive or any officer authorised in writing by the Chief Executive to compound offences under section 54 of the Act; 'offer to compound' means a written offer made by a compounding officer under regulation 4.
provisions.explanation: Defines the key terms. The concept of a 'compounding officer' allows the Chief Executive to delegate the compounding function to authorised subordinates. Note: original file referenced 'section 55' (protection against suits) — corrected to 'section 54' (compounding power).

provisions.title: List of Compoundable Offences
provisions.requirement: The following offences under the Act are prescribed as compoundable offences: (a) failure to comply with a direction to implement measures for cyber security under section 24, punishable under section 24(3); (b) failure to notify a cyber security incident within the prescribed time under section 26, punishable under section 26(3); (c) failure to cause a risk assessment to be conducted within the prescribed interval under section 21, punishable under section 21(4); (d) failure to cause an audit to be conducted within the prescribed interval under section 23, punishable under section 23(4); (e) failure to submit a risk assessment report or audit report within the prescribed time under the Risk Assessment and Audit Regulations (P.U.(A) 219/2024), regulation 14 [sub-section reference requires gazette verification]; (f) failure to comply with a direction following a risk assessment or audit under section 21 or 23 of the Act [sub-section reference requires gazette verification]; (g) provision of cyber security services without a valid licence under section 28, punishable under section 28(3), where the contravention is a first offence and the service was not provided to a national critical information infrastructure entity; (h) failure to comply with a condition of a cyber security service provider licence under section 32, punishable under section 32(2); (i) failure to comply with a code of practice under section 22, punishable under section 22(4); (j) obstruction of an authorised officer in the exercise of powers under section 44, punishable under section 44, where the obstruction does not involve physical force or threats. The following offences are NOT compoundable: offences involving false statements, offences under section 49 (obligation of secrecy), and provision of unlicensed services under section 28(3) where the unlicensed service was provided to a national critical information infrastructure entity.
provisions.explanation: Corrected 2026-03-04: Original file contained multiple wrong section references throughout. Key corrections: risk assessment (s21, not s27), audit (s23, not s28), obstruction (s44, not s43), codes of practice (s22, not s36). Items (e) and (f) in the original cited s29 and s30 (Part V licensing provisions) which were entirely wrong — corrected to reflect the risk assessment/audit regulatory obligations. Penalty sub-sections for items (e) and (f) require gazette verification. The list of non-compoundable offences has also been generalised pending verification of exact section references.
provisions.verificationStatus: section-references-corrected; penalty sub-sections for items (e) and (f) require gazette verification

provisions.title: Offer to Compound
provisions.requirement: Where the compounding officer has reasonable grounds to believe that a person has committed a compoundable offence, the compounding officer may make a written offer to compound the offence. The offer to compound shall state: (a) the name and address of the person to whom the offer is made; (b) the nature of the offence and the provision of the Act contravened; (c) the date, time, and circumstances of the offence; (d) the compound amount payable; (e) the basis for calculating the compound amount under regulation 5; (f) the period within which the compound amount must be paid, which shall not be less than thirty days from the date of the offer; (g) the consequences of accepting or rejecting the offer, including that rejection may result in criminal prosecution; (h) the method of payment. The offer to compound may be made at any time before a charge has been preferred in court for the offence. An offer to compound shall not be construed as an admission or finding of guilt.
provisions.explanation: The offer to compound is an administrative alternative to criminal prosecution, providing efficiency benefits for both NACSA and the alleged offender. The requirement to specify the calculation basis ensures transparency. Critically, the offer can only be made before formal charges are filed, after which the matter must proceed through the courts. The explicit statement that an offer does not imply guilt protects the rights of the person to whom the offer is made if they choose to reject it.

provisions.title: Compound Amount Calculation
provisions.requirement: The compound amount for a compoundable offence shall not exceed fifty per centum of the maximum fine prescribed for the offence. In determining the compound amount within this limit, the compounding officer shall have regard to the following factors: (a) the nature and gravity of the offence; (b) the duration of the contravention; (c) whether the contravention was committed deliberately, recklessly, or negligently; (d) whether the person has previously been compounded for or convicted of an offence under the Act; (e) the extent of any harm or potential harm caused by the contravention, including harm to national security, public safety, or the functioning of national critical information infrastructure; (f) the degree to which the person cooperated with NACSA in identifying and rectifying the contravention; (g) the financial position and capacity of the person, including whether the person is an individual or a body corporate; (h) any remedial action taken by the person before the offer to compound was made; (i) any other factor the compounding officer considers relevant. The compounding officer shall record in writing the assessment of each factor and the basis for the compound amount determined.
provisions.explanation: The 50% ceiling means that for an offence carrying a maximum fine of RM500,000, the maximum compound amount is RM250,000. The nine assessment factors provide a structured framework for exercising discretion, promoting consistency across cases while allowing case-specific adjustments. The written assessment requirement creates an audit trail and supports internal quality assurance.

provisions.title: Acceptance and Payment
provisions.requirement: A person who wishes to accept an offer to compound shall, within the period specified in the offer: (a) signify acceptance in writing to the compounding officer; (b) pay the compound amount in full by way of bank draft, electronic transfer, or any other method specified in the offer, payable to the Government of Malaysia. The compound amount shall be paid in a single lump sum and no instalment arrangement shall be permitted. Where payment is made by electronic transfer, the date of payment shall be the date on which the funds are credited to the designated government account. A receipt shall be issued by the compounding officer upon confirmation of payment.
provisions.explanation: The payment procedure is deliberately straightforward: full lump sum payment within the stipulated period, with no instalment plans. The designation of the payment date as the date of receipt (not transmission) places the risk of transfer delays on the payer.

provisions.title: Payment Timelines and Extension
provisions.requirement: The period for payment specified in the offer to compound shall not be less than thirty days and not more than ninety days from the date of the offer. The compounding officer may, upon written application by the person made before the expiry of the payment period, grant one extension of time not exceeding thirty days, where the compounding officer is satisfied that: (a) the person has a genuine intention to pay the compound amount; (b) the delay in payment is due to circumstances beyond the person's reasonable control; (c) granting the extension would not be contrary to the public interest. Where the compound amount is not paid within the period specified in the offer or any extension thereof, the offer to compound shall lapse and the compounding officer may proceed to institute criminal prosecution for the offence.
provisions.explanation: The 30-90 day base payment window provides flexibility to calibrate the urgency based on the gravity of the offence. The single extension of up to 30 days provides a safety valve without creating an open-ended deferral mechanism. Lapse of the offer upon non-payment automatically reverts the matter to the prosecution track.

provisions.title: Effect of Compounding
provisions.requirement: Where a compoundable offence has been compounded under these Regulations: (a) no prosecution shall thereafter be instituted in respect of the offence against the person to whom the offer was made; (b) any document or thing seized in connection with the offence shall be released to the person from whom it was seized, unless it is required for the investigation or prosecution of any other offence; (c) the compounding shall not be treated as a conviction for the purposes of any written law; (d) the compounding shall, however, be recorded by the Chief Executive and may be taken into account as a factor under regulation 5(d) in any subsequent compounding offer, and may be taken into account by the Chief Executive in any decision relating to the licensing or registration of the person under the Act; (e) the compounding shall not affect any right of a third party to bring a civil action in respect of any loss or damage arising from the conduct that constituted the offence.
provisions.explanation: The legal effect of compounding is carefully delineated. It provides finality against prosecution for that specific offence (paragraph a) and is not a criminal conviction (paragraph c). However, the compounding is recorded and can influence future compound amounts and licensing decisions (paragraph d). The preservation of third-party civil rights (paragraph e) ensures that compounding between NACSA and the offender does not prejudice persons who suffered harm.

provisions.title: Register of Compounded Offences
provisions.requirement: The Chief Executive shall maintain a register of all offences compounded under these Regulations, which shall contain: (a) the name and identification of the person whose offence was compounded; (b) the nature of the offence compounded and the provision of the Act contravened; (c) the date of the offence and the date of the offer to compound; (d) the compound amount paid and the date of payment; (e) the factors considered in determining the compound amount. The register shall not be published or made available to the general public but shall be available to compounding officers for the purposes of regulation 5(d) and to the Chief Executive for the purposes of licensing and registration decisions under the Act. The register shall be maintained for a period of not less than ten years from the date of each entry.
provisions.explanation: Unlike the public registers for assessors, auditors, and licensees, the compounding register is confidential. This reflects the policy that compounding is not a conviction and should not result in public shaming. However, the register is accessible internally to inform future compounding decisions and licensing assessments. The 10-year retention period reflects the significance of enforcement history.

provisions.title: Withdrawal of Offer
provisions.requirement: The compounding officer may withdraw an offer to compound at any time before the compound amount is paid if: (a) the compounding officer becomes aware of new information that materially affects the nature or gravity of the offence; (b) the person to whom the offer was made has committed a further offence under the Act; (c) the compounding officer is satisfied that the offer was made based on incomplete or inaccurate information. The withdrawal of an offer to compound shall be communicated in writing to the person and shall state the reasons for the withdrawal. Upon withdrawal, the compounding officer may make a fresh offer to compound at a different compound amount, or may proceed to institute criminal prosecution for the offence.
provisions.explanation: The withdrawal power is necessary to address situations where the original compounding assessment was based on incomplete information. The requirement to provide written reasons ensures transparency and provides a basis for any challenge to the withdrawal.

provisions.title: Right to Reject and Consequences
provisions.requirement: A person to whom an offer to compound is made is under no obligation to accept the offer. Where a person rejects the offer or fails to respond within the period specified in the offer, the offer shall lapse and the compounding officer may proceed to institute criminal prosecution for the offence. The rejection of an offer to compound, the making of an offer to compound, and any communications between the compounding officer and the person in the course of the compounding process shall not be admissible as evidence in any subsequent criminal proceedings for the offence.
provisions.explanation: This provision protects the voluntary nature of compounding by ensuring that the decision to reject carries no evidential prejudice in subsequent criminal proceedings. A person who believes they are innocent can decline the offer and contest the matter in court without the offer or any related communications being used against them.

provisions.title: Allocation of Compound Amounts
provisions.requirement: All compound amounts received under these Regulations shall be paid into the Federal Consolidated Fund in accordance with the Financial Procedure Act 1957. The Chief Executive shall maintain accurate financial records of all compound amounts received and shall include in the annual report submitted under the relevant provision of the Act a summary of all offences compounded during the year, including the total number of offers made, offers accepted, offers rejected or lapsed, and the total compound amounts collected.
provisions.explanation: Compound amounts are public revenue and must be channelled through the proper government financial system. The annual reporting requirement ensures parliamentary and public oversight of NACSA's compounding activities. Note: original file referenced 'section 58 of the Act' for the annual report obligation — s58 is the transitional provision for cyber security service providers; the exact annual report provision requires gazette verification.
provisions.verificationStatus: annual-report-section-unverified: original file cited s58 (transitional provision) — verify correct section against official gazette

notes: This content is indicative and does not reproduce the official gazette text. Specific figures (e.g. fine amounts, timelines, insurance thresholds, fees) are examples based on publicly available information and may differ from the actual subsidiary legislation. Always refer to the official P.U.(A) gazette for authoritative requirements.
